---
title: "Disaggregated Inference: Architecture, Tradeoffs, and Trends"
date: "2026-07-05"
categories: [LLM, inference, disaggregated inference, systems]
draft: true
---

Disaggregated Inference (DI), also known as disaggregated serving, disaggregated prefill, or P/D disaggregation, is an LLM serving architecture that separates the prefill and decode phases of inference onto different hardware resources. Compared to other LLM inference techniques, disaggregated inference is a pretty new and trendy paradigm. As this is a moving area, in this post I will cover the higher-level conceptual view of DI, considerations for its usage in production systems, and recent trends as I see them.

## Quick Basics and Motivation

LLM inference has distinct requirements in its two phases of execution: prefill and decode. A quick recap: the prefill phase happens when the LLM processes all input tokens together -- it is compute-heavy and relies on dense matrix-matrix multiplication. Modern GPU kernels have been gradually improved over the last few years to tackle the compute challenge of the prefill phase. The decode phase, when the LLM generates one token at a time, becomes extremely memory-bound, because for every token the LLM needs to read all weights from memory. This phase has very low compute density as it relies on matrix-vector multiplication.

Another noteworthy point is that inference providers and enterprises are extremely cautious about token cost. To reduce the cost of tokens, high-throughput serving is required -- but that hurts interactivity, or the individual user's experience in terms of tokens/sec they receive. The pareto curve shows that higher interactivity demands lower batch size, and that increases cost. So the industry is deeply trying to find ways to improve interactivity (tokens/sec/user) while keeping cost lower and throughput higher.

The primary benefit of disaggregated serving is improving interactivity. When both phases of the LLM are colocated on the same GPU, the decode phase can suffer from the prefill phase of incoming new requests, as continuous batching allows new requests to always fill in the stream. Each request in the decode batch can get interrupted -- called "Head-of-Line" blocking -- causing irregular ITL (inter-token latency) and degraded interactivity. By disaggregating, the decode phase can happen in its own decode GPU pool, achieving a steady-state, stable ITL and improving throughput.

With the primary benefit stated above, there are additional benefits in a disaggregated setup:

1. **Independent optimization and scaling** -- since prefill workers and decode workers are separated, they can often scale independently to maximize throughput based on varying workload. This enables independent parallelism, batching, and P:D ratio flexibility.
2. **Heterogeneous accelerators** -- disaggregation provides even more flexibility where different types of accelerators can be used, which might be a better fit for compute-bound vs. memory-bandwidth-bound work.

## Architectural Considerations of GPU-GPU Disaggregated Inference

With the basics covered, let's look at the main components of disaggregated serving. It is worth noting that while disaggregated inference can be done within a node (for example, 4 GPUs for prefill and 4 GPUs for decode), I will mainly discuss disaggregated inference at the cluster level.

In a distributed inference system, there are traditionally two main components: the router, which works in the request/data plane between requests and servers, and the autoscaler, which works in the control plane to scale the servers. In disaggregated inference we have further granularity: prefill clusters are dedicated prefill servers to run the prefill workload, decode clusters are decode servers to run the decode workload, and KV connectors or transfer engines are data-plane movers from the prefill servers to the decode servers.

### Dynamic xPyD

Disaggregated inference scaling imposes scaling requirements on both prefill and decode clusters. Prefill-heavy workloads such as summarization tasks and large document processing can cause fewer requests to be processed on the prefill servers compared to the decode servers, creating a bottleneck limited by the number of prefill servers even if decode servers are underutilized. On the other hand, for decode-heavy workloads such as long reasoning tasks, decode servers can become the bottleneck. A static scaling such as a 1:1 ratio of prefill to decode often leads to hardware underutilization and suboptimal throughput. Distributed inference frameworks such as NVIDIA Dynamo (SLA-based planner) and llm-d (WVA, Workload Variant Autoscaler) watch signals like prefill queue depth, decode-side KV cache usage, and SLO targets, and scale the prefill and decode servers accordingly.

### Co-scheduling in Disaggregated Inference

Disaggregated inference introduces a co-scheduling problem: prefill and decode workers are separate but tightly coupled, so their placement, scaling, and routing all need to be coordinated together rather than treated independently.

1. **Physical proximity** -- prefill and decode workers need to be scheduled in close physical proximity (same rack, zone, etc.) so that KV cache transfers between them are fast and low-latency. This can be handled by topology-aware gang scheduling.
2. **KV cache transfer efficiency** -- the scheduling system needs to be aware of the KV cache transfer cost and route requests to decode workers that are topologically close to the selected prefill worker, maximizing cache transfer performance.
3. **Balancing the ratio of prefill vs. decode workers** -- the scheduler needs to independently scale prefill and decode worker counts based on live traffic and SLA targets (TTFT and TPOT). For example, it can skip disaggregation and fall back to colocated inference for small loads.

### Fallback to Colocation

One important caveat: disaggregation does not always pay off for every request. An intelligent router can assess this and route a request entirely to a decode server that does both prefill and decode and generates all tokens. This can happen under the following conditions:

1. If the prefill length of a request is short, it can be efficiently computed in the decode engine by piggybacking chunked prefill requests with ongoing decode requests, eliminating the need for small KV transfer overhead.
2. **Prefix cache hit** -- prefill becomes memory-bound when a prefix cache hit occurs on the decode servers, and hence can be more efficiently computed in the decode engine.
3. If the prefill queue is overloaded with a long queue, it could be better to serve the request on the decode servers until more workers are allocated to the prefill queue.

## The Transport Layer: KV Cache Movement

The KV cache transfer from prefill clusters to decode clusters is critical -- a slow transfer path wipes out the benefits of disaggregation. Inference engines such as vLLM and SGLang, and distributed inference frameworks such as Dynamo and llm-d, all tend to use one or more high-performance data transfer libraries such as Nvidia's NIXL and AMD's MoRI-IO. These libraries avoid CPU staging (copying from source GPU HBM to host RAM over PCIe before transferring to the destination GPU), and use asynchronous transfer over the ultra-fast interconnect fabric to make it as efficient as possible.

In Dynamo, the decode-side worker reserves KV cache blocks in its GPU memory and passes the corresponding memory descriptor to the prefill worker. The prefill worker then uses the NIXL transfer library, over RDMA-capable networking such as InfiniBand or RoCE, to access those decode-side GPU KV blocks directly. It may read existing prefix-hit KV blocks from the decode GPU, compute the missing KV cache, and write the newly computed KV blocks into the decode GPU's reserved KV memory. These KV block reads and writes are completely asynchronous so that both prefill and decode workers can serve other requests while transfers are in-flight.

MoRI-IO is AMD's native transfer library optimized for KV cache movement in disaggregated inference, integrated into inference engines such as vLLM and SGLang. In vLLM, it is exposed through the MoRI-IO connector. It provides an optimized path to move KV cache between prefill and decode workers, with support for both decode-pull READ mode and prefill-push WRITE mode. WRITE mode is the default and usually faster because the decode side is prepared early and the prefill side can push KV blocks as they are produced, instead of waiting for the full prefill to finish before starting the KV transfer. Depending on the deployment topology, MoRI-IO can use GPU-to-GPU transfer paths such as xGMI inside a node, or RDMA-capable networking across nodes.

For sparse-attention models such as DeepSeek-V3 with DeepSeek Sparse Attention (DSA), KV access can become much more fine-grained because the decoder only needs selected KV entries. This makes CXL-backed KV memory pooling an interesting emerging direction, where KV can stay in a shared memory pool and be accessed on demand instead of relying only on RDMA-style KV transfer.

## KV Cache Management with Disaggregated Inference

In a disaggregated inference setup where many servers are running, reusing the KV cache efficiently across all servers becomes highly important. A KV cache management layer such as LMCache turns KV cache from a temporary artifact into a reusable knowledge base that can be stored persistently and reused across multiple serving engines. It moves KV caches out of GPU memory into a tiered storage hierarchy spanning CPU memory, local storage, and remote storage backends. This provides a decoupled handoff option instead of a strict point-to-point transfer from prefill worker to decode worker. KV offloading from decode workers also helps maintain high throughput.

In today's agentic workloads with massive documents, long history, and multi-turn conversations, the generated KV cache can be reused using this approach, since prefill worker compute can be avoided. Mooncake Store can provide a distributed backend for that cache, functioning as a high-performance remote storage engine for KV cache: deciding how to store and retrieve it quickly and reliably. LMCache then manages the KV cache on top of this storage layer.

## Beyond Traditional Disaggregation

The disaggregation of prefill and decode phases opens new opportunities to use entirely different accelerators for the decode phase -- ones that could be a better fit for memory-bound operation than GPUs. Recently, SRAM-centric accelerators have become an active research area for this. GPUs need to read all weights and KV cache from off-chip HBM to generate one token at a time, causing a significant memory bottleneck. SRAM-centric accelerators, on the other hand, are well-suited for memory bandwidth because they rely on fast on-chip SRAM to store data. These accelerators avoid the transfer delay from off-chip memory to on-chip memory. As on-chip SRAM in these accelerators is typically wider in access paths and distributed across the chip, it provides a faster compute path, delivering low latency in the decode phase where GPUs are typically constrained by memory bandwidth.

In the first half of 2026, I have observed multiple announcements and trends related to heterogeneous disaggregated inference. I find these genuinely interesting and am curious how all these solutions will pan out. Here are a few highlights:

**AFD (Attention Feedforward Disaggregation)** -- Nvidia announced AFD where the decode phase's feedforward layers are accelerated with Groq's LPU. Every layer in an autoregressive LLM consists of attention followed by feedforward layers. The attention part needs to read and write to a growing KV cache, so that part stays on the GPU. After attention, the feedforward path does not need KV cache -- it only needs the output of the attention block -- and this is accelerated on the LPU. This repeats in every layer.

**Speculative decoding disaggregation** -- speculative decoding has a draft phase (memory-bound) and a verification stage (compute-bound). This asymmetry can also be exploited by disaggregation, with the draft and verification stages running on hardware tuned for each workload.

**AWS llm-d** -- AWS announced disaggregated inference on AWS powered by llm-d: https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/introducing-disaggregated-inference-on-aws-powered-by-llm-d/
